item.title: My solution to the "Dead" riddle
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the clues from everyone trying to solve this. Here's my stab at it (no pun intended.)

Note: I have totally revised my first guess since trying to solve this yesterday, after deciding that a couple of details made my original theory impossible.

+ show block
– hide block

The baby teeth ("deciduous teeth") have been swallowed. There are missing teeth, "forcibly removed"; the remaining teeth are only the "foreign teeth". "Foreign teeth" is not a dental term, and PeppersGhost mentioned this as being important, ie, no accident. So while the body is a genetic match for every other instance (as are the baby teeth in the stomach), the teeth are uniquely not.

There is a deep cut to the forearm and "scratches" to the bone - something has been dug out of the arm. The nails have been removed. The gingiva around the teeth has been scraped away, and possibly also the cuticles. Hair has been "threaded and sewn in". Teeth, nails, and hair are all "dead" cells; do they have something in common that is being affected? Note also the title of the SCP is "dead".

Theory 2.0: Could this be a body at war with itself - trying repeatedly to kill itself (and succeeding)? It is transforming, becoming someone else, and the original parts are rejecting the new ones - much as an immune system rejects a foreign organ. Attacking it.

As the transformation moves through the living cells, turning them into another copy of 4107, the dead ones are left unchanged - still bearing the imprint of the original host. The woman cuts her cuticles to more easily pull her nails - until she does, she is fighting the nails whenever she tries to move her hands, and is at risk of being scratched by them. She loosens the original host's teeth (now foreign in her new mouth) with the knife used to cut her cuticles, then pulls as many as she can with something like pliers. Some teeth break at the roots, some are pulled and discarded, others which cannot reach her tongue or do damage she ignores.

At this point she has a little time to change clothes, perhaps to eat something. But as the dead tissues further animate to fight back, the original host's hair threads itself through her upper back. She is incapacitated, as she is unable to reach her own back to effectively free herself. While unable to keep an eye on the malicious discarded cells, a fingernail works its way into her arm, down to the bone, and scratches at the ulnar nerve. In desperation she cuts it out, and dies of blood loss.

Her death restarts the cycle a few months later, with another random person becoming an instance of her. Because she cannot remember her previous lives - or she would be better able to defend against these attacks - she is doomed to keep repeating the same cycle of incarnation, war with self, and accidental death.

The "circumstances of death" have been determined in many cases, and are the same. This is not exactly "suicide", but the exsanguination was "self-inflicted". It is unthinkable that all of these wounds were "self-inflicted", and yet they were, because she is more than one "self".

The only detail not explained by this theory is the baby teeth. I don't know how she would get hold of them when reincarnating - or perhaps turn the lost "foreign" teeth back into her own "deciduous" teeth once removed. One idea that interests me is that if she did somehow have some of her own teeth from her childhood, perhaps she was in a state of confusion and trying to replace the hostile teeth with her own teeth. Which did not, of course, work - teeth can't be replanted like trees - so they were left to lie in her mouth or be swallowed.

/end long rambling theory which is the best I have… again, for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is real confusing! The precision of the loop event seems like a ritualistic event, triggered by the specific torture and death of this woman, presumably by her younger self? Also, the scratches on the bone imply runes of some sort. I don't quite understand the motive, however, or more importantly, where the woman's adult teeth went.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have to agree with prior posts about the lack of resolution being a problem, at least for me. I think the difference between this and other SCP's that lack resolution is that this one explicitly has the article asking people to infer… something, and there's a frustrating lack of ability to even check if one's inference is right. If it didn't have the last two paragraphs, I'd feel like I could upvote it, but as it stands I can't really say I enjoyed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This would be so much easier to figure out in-universe than it is for us. Just say the possibilities out loud until there's one you can't.

"The suicide of SCP-4107… Nope, not that.
The time-looping of SCP-4107… Not that either.
The—
…
Okay, I've got it."

The wounds described are the kind of thing that are blatantly the result of torture, and yet the possibility isn't raised anywhere in the description. Within the context of the article itself, there's no reason to think it'd be anything other than that… But it doesn't jive with the other hints, in particular the use of the "loop" tag and the author's comments here.

So I really have no idea. PeppersGhost, if you're still reading this, would you mind filling me in? I know it's not supposed to have a single possible answer, but I'd still like to know the answer you had in mind, at least.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
why would a person’s own baby teeth be considered “foreign” teeth?

This is the correct question to be asking.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know this is a bit old, but I as assumed that “foreign teeth” referred to fillings/crowns/etc.. After reading this closely I think I’m probably wrong, but why would a person’s own baby teeth be considered “foreign” teeth?

I feel like I’m just being foolish in not understanding here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It just is confusing, hard to understand. And also very disturbing, very well written Peppery guy. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i solved the case: she tripped and fell and gave herself a lot of booboos

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think MobRules said what you just said a lot more constructively.

Just to keep it in 1 comment,

+ The Spoilz(?)
- oh no the spoilz(?)

Can't be kill or murder. That's one of 2 popular theories but "murder suspect" was literally said in the article.

I'd agree with the other popular theory of "suicide." 1 corpse is missing teeth and has baby teeth in its throat. A child 4107 killed an older one and put her own teeth in the victim. Then the child grows older and has her adult teeth pulled out. It matches the "loop" tag.

It must've been voluntary for both parties as the older one changed her clothes afterwards. She isn't lying on a pool of blood so it mustve been cleaned. And this can be done entirely discrete somewhere private so the child never gets caught, but how reliably each child can make no mistake and get away is questionable. Especially since all instances must live without leaving a record of themselves.

The frequency of deaths is 311 bodies divided by (2019-1902.5) years is an average of 4.5 months per death. That's… not enough time for each iteration's child to grow into a woman. It might be that there are multiple instances of older and younger 4107s making up multiple chains. Well that hurts my head so I'm gonna stop right there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My dumb guess is "they can't call it a murder and they can't call it a suicide because technically it's a selficide, and we don't have a word for selficide."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First of all, an amputee lynx spider with acrophobia in the desert.

Second.
– hide block

holding it above our heads like an empty fist above a dog who thinks it holds a treat

Vote however you like, but I'm honestly pretty offended that you would characterize me this way. I have explicitly stated my intentions here multiple times and encouraged people to find their own answers. I don't regret it either, since many of the theories posited have been incredibly interesting and never would have occurred to me, and they're honestly equally as valid explanations as my authorial headcanon is.

Lewis Carroll wrote "Why is a raven like a writing desk?" to be a nonsense riddle. However, "Poe wrote on both" is a perfectly valid answer to the riddle, even though it didn't come from the author. In the same way, I argue that the existence of the explanations offered by the other commenters here shows that your accusation of there being no answer is patently false.

I'd liken this more to Sampson's riddle—the answer is technically valid and plausible to guess with the limited information given, but only through either sheer luck or wild mass guessing. The difference here is that I haven't offered anyone any garments as prizes. I've straight up told people that the piece exists to provoke conjecture purely for conjecture's sake. Personally, I enjoy forming theories as reader, and this was written for like-minded folks.

Furthermore, this is far from the first article to withhold explanations for what's going on. Sometimes authors provide answers in the comments or The Leak, but not always. If you're simply pissed off because I chose not to do so, then you're not voting based on content.

The point of this isn't to be unsolvable or to laugh in the face of people for not deducing the arbitrary contrivances I've images. The point is to offer a story where you can fill in whatever explanation you like.

I said in the first post that I have an answer in mind. I made that backtory so I'd have a general framework to work from and keep things internally consistent. Either you ignored that post before making accusations, or you straight up don't believe me. If you really want to go as far as calling me a liar to my face, I will tell you the backstory I was working from when I wrote this. However, I predict the response would be something along the lines of "that answer's stupid", to which I will preemptively point out that's why I said it wasn't important.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"What's green, has five legs, is warm to the touch and doesn't like ladders?"

I read this article when it came out and couldn't solve it then, though I did try.

To come up with all these components of a mystery, all these little threads that don't quite tie together… it feels like cheating, to me. I see a lot of first time authors write articles that have a bunch of different things and then say "well, you work it out" or "it's anomalous" to excuse it, because they can't find a connection either. Using that concept in an article, and then either hiding the answer or holding it above our heads like an empty fist above a dog who thinks it holds a treat, feels off to me.

It's easy to come up with a riddle that doesn't have an answer. -1

Thanks to everyone who has expressed interest in the mystery here! It wasn't originally intended to be a puzzlebox, but folks seem to have gotten really invested in trying to figure out what backstory I had in mind, so it seems only right to offer at least a little closure. Since the article's been out for more than a month, now seems like a good time for an update.

Before I get into the details, I'll reiterate that the point of the article has always been the mystery itself, not the solution that may be behind it, so I can't promise you'll be satisfied if you're after concrete answers.

Spoilers(?)
– hide block

At least one of the theories posted on this thread has correctly guessed the general explanation I had in mind. I don't think anyone's given an all-inclusive answer that accounts for all the little details (such as the injuries), but honestly those details are even more obtuse and a lot less important than the question of whodunnit.

There's another layer to this article, too. In the original draft, the corpse was an infohazard which made people come up with numerous different wild backstories for how the woman died. For my version, I wanted to achieve a similar effect in real life, creating a compulsion effect that exists both in and out of universe without ever mentioning it directly. It's fun to know I've succeeded to a degree!

Not necessarily hints, but some stray thoughts:

  • The body was mutilated, so one would normally expect the photograph to show it lying in a pool of blood.
  • There is at least one tag that I chose not to list in order to keep the mystery open-ended.
  • The end of the article mentions one specific action, but there's more than one way to look at it.
  • The aforementioned compulsion effect is mostly an easter egg and isn't necessarily tied into the mystery.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
However, the deciduous teeth mentioned in the article are stated as to be those of the instance, so I think it would clear things up to change:

  • All non-foreign teeth have been forcibly removed, though several roots are still present within the jaw, presumably broken off during removal. The surrounding gingiva has been scraped from the mandible and maxilla with the edge of a serrated blade.
  • A loose collection of deciduous teeth and teeth fragments can be found inside the mouth and esophagus. The teeth are a genetic match for SCP-4107, and are of a consistent age to have plausibly belonged to the victim during their childhood.

Into this:

  • All secondary1 teeth have been forcibly removed, though several roots are still present within the jaw, presumably broken off during removal. The surrounding gingiva has been scraped from the mandible and maxilla with the blade of a serrated implement.
  • A loose collection of deciduous teeth2 and assorted tooth fragments3 can be found inside the mouth and esophagus.
Footnotes
1. Also referred to as permanent or 'adult' teeth.
2. Commonly referred to as primary or 'baby' teeth.
3. All dental material present matches the genetic profile of SCP-4107, and all deciduous teeth are of consistent age and wear to have plausibly belonged to the instance during childhood.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this is an amazing entry, and a wonderful concept.

I think the reason it’s hard to discern the missing information is that all SCP entries are missing information that really should be there. No SCP entry would be acceptable as an actual government document. The thrill of SCP wiki is piecing together the fragments of story.

It’s the same thing here. There may indeed be something missing, but it’s so difficult to infer because we don’t have actual, “complete” SCP entries to refer to.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was my first thought as well.

+ Thoughts
- Hide

If the aforementioned action is suicide, then perhaps one of the victims final acts was an antimemetic echo against the concept of suicide. She broadcast the idea, "I didn't kill myself, someone did this to me! Find them!!" so powerfully that her crime scene keeps showing up over and over again, and also no one is allowed to even infer that it might have been suicide.

One more random thought:

A loose collection of deciduous teeth and teeth fragments can be found inside the mouth and esophagus. The teeth are a genetic match for SCP-4107, and are of a consistent age to have plausibly belonged to the victim during their childhood.

Why did the attacker try and put her baby teeth back in her mouth? Where did her attacker even GET her baby teeth? My mother has kept some of my baby teeth in her drawer, so in my mind, the only person who would have access to someone else's baby teeth would be that person's parents. That tells me that this wasn't sexual or a jilted lover or a random act of violence, this was the action of a parent against their child.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Still not quite sure what to think of this, but I have an idea.

+ My current thoughts
- could be completely off base
  • The word "kill" or any conjugations of it aren't found in the text. Often this leads to purposely awkward phrasing. Some examples:

Thus far, researchers have been unable to ascertain when the original death took place, or if there was an originating event at all.

Cause of death has been ruled as blood loss. All injuries are believed to have been inflicted antemortem. In all cases, evidence has indicated that the victim changed clothes between the time most of the injuries were inflicted and the moment of death.

  • Many of the injuries seem to have been likely to cause death, so that they all occurred antemortem is telling. Scratching your arm when it's already been severed to the bone, as an example. Or swallowing your teeth after they have all been bashed out and scraped away.

So, my current thought: the one specific action is killing. This woman, whoever she is, cannot be permanently killed. She is killed and killed and killed, but there is some sort of "save point" at where the point of death would be, after which she regenerates – and this is the key – without healing. Perhaps the first death was a murder, but the other deaths have been her futile attempts at suicide. She wants to die, but cannot, because when she tries to kill herself the same effect occurs. She constantly lives at the brink of death, and can never escape.

I thought about this SCP a lot, and I'm aware I could be completely off, but I still love any SCP that makes me think this much. Take your upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But the lack of murder suspects is mentioned in the article so I don’t think that can be it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Made me think and is very well written, but I can’t bring myself to upvote for now since I won’t know if this is a satisfying mystery or not until someone solves it.

The baby teeth in the mouth is the most intriguing part, makes me wonder if somehow the body was never properly alive or conscious somehow and the baby teeth were pushed out but never digested or fully swallowed, but the rest of the article doesn’t seem to support that.

The draft this is based on makes it seem like suicide is the action that can’t be mentioned, but I’m not sure if that’s it, since I can’t see what information is obviously missing from the document that would allow us to infer that. If that is what the action is it can’t be the whole story.

There’s definitely something going on here and I think that, as someone has suggested, the apparent connection between the wounds is important.

Edit: still puzzling over this hours later so I think I will upvote after all since it’s had such an impact. Think I’m stumped though. Been trying to figure out the action based on what information is missing or based on what would be most horrific if true, but so far no luck. Can we have a clue?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, but I want ore information, probably here in the comments. no vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm having a hard time making heads or tails of this.

some thoughts...
– hide block

I suspect that the "specific action" that should be inferred from what's not mentioned is murder. They never say they suspect it was murder, they never say they suspect it wasn't.

But without the big picture, that just seems like a random bit added for no reason. Actually, without the big picture it ALL seems like random bits added for no reason. There's no coherence… or there may be, but it's hidden.

The thing about the incision on the arm seems very specific and very random (removing embedded technology?). The hair thing seems random. The thing about there being a "specific act" that cannot be mentioned seems random. If I'm correct that it's murder, that also seems random.

I tried to look at the tags for hints:

  • Antimemetic: obviously the "there's something we can't mention" thing, and the bit about how there are never any witnesses and no one has memory or record of her being alive. But no way of knowing how important the antimemetic component is, how deep it goes, or whether there's more to it.
  • Cadaver, Euclid, Humanoid: all pretty obvious
  • Loop: interesting. There's multiple copies because she's in a time-loop somehow? Still not seeing how that would work, though.
  • Meta: very interesting. But I'm completely missing what it's significance is. Another random bit that doesn't feel connected to the other random bits.

For all I know, this may be the cleverest piece of writing on the site for those that can figure it out. But to me, it's just a random pile of disconnected bits with no cohesiveness whatsoever.

Since I have to vote on what I can see, and not on what might be there but that I'm not clever enough to discern, this is going to have to be a downvote. This might change, if I get a breakthrough and it starts making any sort of sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know what's going on, but I am very disturbed by it.

+ Theories
- Ugh, I'm sad now

I don't think it's suicide, because some of the wounds can't reasonably be self-inflicted, such as the hair being sewn to the back. My mind immediately jumps to violent torture, that may include rape. Removing teeth and nails would prevent her from fighting back, even a little. I think the nature of Jane Doe's death was so violent that her body keeps reappearing in an effort to force someone, anyone, to solve the mystery and avenge her. Sort of like how a ghost can't leave until it completes its earthly business.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't say I get what the specific action is supposed to be, but the short nature of the article hits that good middle point of not saying what the hell is going and giving just enough information to point the right way.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
along with that part about "non-foreign teeth" is bugging me. Why would there be foreign teeth present in the first place?

Because the presence of foreign teeth is mentioned in that same paragraph

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's what I had thought, too! I learn a lot when doing SCP research.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really want to like this, but the lack of any clear connection between the different wounds or what their purpose is, along with that part about "non-foreign teeth" is bugging me. Why would there be foreign teeth present in the first place?

Without any obvious reason behind them, the wounds just feel random, with the only intention behind them being to be weird.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought it was "premortem" and "postmortem", not "antemortem". Although autocorrect seems to disagree so never mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I read it when I was half-asleep, so didn't occupy a state of mind to offer good, solid feedback — that being said, it's still excellent. It does an great job of balancing on that knife's edge between "enough to make you think something's going on" vs "not enough to know what".

The prose is also deliciously evocative of raw, harrowing body-horror (hard to do these days, especially in the medium of text). Short, punchy, just the right amount of restraint — and splendidly creepy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've got some ideas. The article has the 'loop' tag, so it's probably a single killing that perpetuates itself, not a phenomenon that occurs for multiple people in isolated instances. The concept that it can't be associated with is suicide, which was fairly easy to pick up on after reading the old draft. It also fits with the loop. She's able to repeatedly kill herself, but some instance of her remains, which will later be killed by the next instance, etc. I assume that's because two of her are there at any given time, one younger and one older.

The other thing I noticed but couldn't interpret about the article itself is that her injuries relate to each other. She's been cut to the bone and scratched internally by fingernails, and her fingernails have been ripped out. She has fragmented child's teeth in her throat, and messily removed adult teeth in her mouth. I don't know what the hair means. I also think that the fact she's named 'Jane Doe' is relevant to whatever happened. She's not an interesting or accomplished person.

As for the song 'Dead', I prefer the original They Might Be Giants version over the cover PeppersGhost linked. But aside from that, here are the lines from that song which may be relevant. I feel like there's something obvious I'm missing, like I'm so close.

"Did a large procession wave their torches as my head fell in the basket, and was everybody dancing on the casket?"
How many instances of her are there at once? Many corpses have been discovered, but when she dies is there a 'large procession'?

"Now it's over, I'm dead and I haven't done anything that I want, or I'm still alive and there's nothing I want to do."
This is where the title of the song, and the article, is mentioned. I think in this case, it's not or, but and.

"I returned a bag of groceries accidentally taken off the shelf before the expiration date. I came back as a bag of groceries accidentally taken off the shelf before the date stamped on myself."
Taken in the context of the article, this could definitely be a hint towards the nature of the loop.

Lastly, I'll just leave this speculation about the song here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah okay you got me. I haven't been this intrigued since the Department of Abnormalities.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yet again, PeppersGhost manages to get my gears turning. I'll be thinking about this one for quite some time.

Also, I'm gonna make an (un)educated guess as to what is going on here

+ Spoiler?
- Definitely not but still...

I have no fucking clue what we can't talk about. Gimme time to think on it.
Theory 1: There was only one murder, and the bodies keep appearing.
Theory 2: The murders happen to different people and something changes the bodies into the same person postmortem (maybe has something to do with the baby teeth)
Will add more as I think of them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey Foundation
is this keter?

Yup

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
this is mind-blogging, written very well and atmospheric despite its shortness. take your updoot +1

for all that are scrolling down the new posts, yes, this is worth a read!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As with most skips, I have no fucking clue what this is. But it's written really well so +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I still don't really get what's going on here, but the imagery and the hints are just distressing enough to get my upvote.

I do have another guess...
– hide block

So after reading the original draft it makes me think that she somehow committed suicide, especially given the fact that she changed clothes between the infliction of the injuries and the time of death, but I can't comprehend how someone would physically be able to harm themself in these specific ways.

item.title: Dead
item.content:encoded:
Did a large procession wave their torches
As my head fell in the basket
And was everybody dancing on the casket?

I do have a concrete explanation in mind, but I think the piece works best if you come to your own conclusions.

Adapted from a self-deleted JamCon skip by OthellotheCat.
Part of Draft Swap, where authors exchange unfinished drafts and breathe new life into them.

Image by Alphonse Bertillon, 1901, Public Domain.

Critters:

  • Captain Kirby - SCP-4775 - The Absolute Gall
  • The Great Hippo - SCP-4089 - Untoothing
  • ch00bakka - SCP-4065 - Ancho(red) Reality
